comparePages.title: Under-sink certified PFAS options are strongest when the route already supports point-of-use treatment
comparePages.lede: This compare page narrows to under-sink certified point-of-use systems that can be defended after a utility dossier, state-guided test, or direct evidence path justifies treatment.
comparePages.nextActionTitle: Compare under-sink systems only after the route has already justified treatment
comparePages.nextActionSummary: Under-sink is often the most practical money path, but it still needs claim scope, annual burden, and installation tolerance to line up with the household route.
comparisonAxes: Upfront price band and annualized maintenance
Whether the system stays proportionate to a drinking-water use case
whyThis: Under-sink is usually the highest-intent point-of-use class for households that accept installation.
The category is broad enough to include simpler carbon systems and higher-burden RO systems.
Comparing under-sink options side by side helps block generic best-filter advice.
whatThisDoesNotTellYou: This page does not prove every under-sink system fits every PFAS scenario.
It does not make certification equal every active or pending benchmark path.
It does not justify whole-house treatment.
evidenceHighlights: EPA filter guidance keeps product review grounded in certification and claim-level verification.
Under-sink options show the clearest tradeoff between carbon simplicity and RO burden.
A stronger compare page reduces generic roundup drift because the lane is tied to the route.
targetQueries: under sink certified pfas filter options

sections.title: Why under-sink becomes the core commercial lane
sections.summary: When the evidence route is real, under-sink is often the cleanest point-of-use class because it combines daily convenience with credible claim review.
paragraphs: Under-sink systems are the point where the project can show actual differences between carbon-led and RO-led ownership models without drifting into a generic roundup voice. That makes the page useful for both SEO intent and conversion quality.
The category is also easier to defend editorially because it maps directly to a drinking-water intervention rather than a whole-home buildout.
bullets: Easier to keep proportionate than whole-house.

sections.title: The useful comparison is claim scope plus burden
sections.summary: Users do not need a loud winner. They need the narrowest system class that still fits the route.
paragraphs: Some under-sink systems stay attractive because they keep annualized ownership lower and installation simpler. Others justify themselves only if the household explicitly prefers RO and accepts the maintenance model. That is why the page keeps cost and cadence in the same frame as claim scope.
The result is a better commercial surface: fewer products, clearer reasons, and less fear-driven upsell pressure.
bullets: The best system is usually the narrowest defendable fit.

sections.title: How this compare page should be used
sections.summary: Treat it as a post-interpretation lane, not an opening move.
paragraphs: The engine should route into this page after a direct utility dossier, a state-guided test interpretation, or a similar evidence-backed decision state. That makes the click path feel rational instead of reactive.
When the route is still low-evidence, the compare lane should remain secondary to testing or utility review.
bullets: Merchant click quality improves when the sequence is respected.

comparePages.title: Countertop vs pitcher vs under-sink for PFAS is mostly a workflow and maintenance decision
comparePages.lede: This compare page focuses on form factor rather than brand prestige, using certified or defensible point-of-use products already linked to the engine.
comparePages.nextActionTitle: Choose a form factor that the household will actually maintain
comparePages.nextActionSummary: The most expensive product class is not automatically the right one. The compare should reward proportionality, maintenance behavior, and real household workflow.
comparisonAxes: Whether the class stays proportionate to the evidence route
whyThis: Form factor often determines whether a product is installed, refilled, and maintained consistently.
The same PFAS question can reasonably end in a pitcher, countertop, or under-sink path.
A dedicated compare surface helps users avoid treating form factor like a prestige ladder.
whatThisDoesNotTellYou: This compare does not declare one class universally best.
It does not erase claim-level differences between exact models.
It does not bypass the evidence step.
evidenceHighlights: EPA guidance supports comparing point-of-use classes before broader interventions.
The engine already has live examples across pitcher, countertop, direct-connect, and under-sink classes.
Operational fit is often as important as upfront price.
targetQueries: countertop vs pitcher vs under sink pfas filter
pitcher or under sink for pfas

sections.title: The wrong question is which class looks most serious
sections.summary: Product classes should be compared by operating fit, not by visual heft or price signaling.
paragraphs: Pitchers, countertop systems, and under-sink systems solve different household constraints. Some minimize installation burden. Others minimize refill friction. Others trade higher commitment for smoother daily use. The compare page works when it makes those tradeoffs visible instead of implying that the most engineered-looking class always wins.
That framing also protects conversion quality because it leads users toward the class they can actually live with.

sections.title: Why pitcher and countertop lanes still matter
sections.summary: Some households need low-plumbing, renter-friendly, or space-constrained options that still keep the intervention narrow.
paragraphs: Pitcher and countertop systems are easy to dismiss in a PFAS conversation because they look smaller and feel less permanent. That is a mistake when the route only justifies a constrained drinking-water intervention and the household wants a lower-friction adoption path.
The page should make those cases visible instead of pushing every user into under-sink by default.
bullets: A lower-commitment class can still be rational.

sections.title: How to use this compare page with the rest of the engine
sections.summary: The page is strongest when paired with live utility examples and related money-path guides.
paragraphs: Users can read the compare table, see how utility dossiers lead into product lanes, and then choose whether the extra installation and maintenance burden of under-sink is actually justified. That keeps the compare page anchored in the same product philosophy as the engine.
It also makes the money path more transparent because the compare page never pretends the click happens in a vacuum.
bullets: Read with annual cost in view.
Treat form factor as operating fit, not prestige.

comparePages.title: PFAS filter annual cost compare shows why upkeep changes the real purchase decision
comparePages.lede: This compare page brings annualized maintenance into the foreground so a lower checkout price cannot hide a worse ownership path.
comparePages.nextActionTitle: Compare yearly ownership before you compare headline price
comparePages.nextActionSummary: A money page gets stronger when it exposes upkeep early. The useful compare is cost over time, not price at checkout alone.
comparisonAxes: Whether the product class stays proportionate to the route
whyThis: Annual cost often explains why a product that looks cheap becomes a poor fit.
The project already normalizes recurring components and cadence, so the compare can be more honest than a simple roundup.
Users need this layer before escalating into more expensive classes.
whatThisDoesNotTellYou: This compare does not assume one universal water usage profile.
It does not make low annual cost more important than claim fit.
It does not justify treatment on its own.
evidenceHighlights: The engine annualizes cadence-based maintenance and recurring component costs where normalized inputs exist.
Cost is presented beside interpretation because maintenance can block otherwise plausible products.
The compare page helps stop high-ticket upsell by making burden visible.

sections.title: Annualized cost is where honest comparison starts
sections.summary: A merchant surface that hides annual burden is weak decision support even if the product list is technically accurate.
paragraphs: The compare should make it easy to see which products stay low-friction over a year and which products demand a stronger maintenance posture. That is more useful than a headline price sort because upkeep is what users actually live with.
This is especially important in PFAS because the strongest-looking unit is often not the most practical one.
bullets: Recurring components change the real burden.
A good compare page makes regret visible before checkout.

sections.title: Use annual cost to block overbuying
sections.summary: Annual cost becomes a restraint, not just a commerce detail.
paragraphs: When the evidence route is still moderate rather than urgent, annualized cost is often the strongest signal against jumping into a premium class that the household does not need. That makes the compare page valuable even when it reduces click volume because it improves click quality.
The engine should therefore keep low-burden point-of-use options visible beside higher-burden systems instead of burying them.
bullets: Cost makes the product lane more honest.
Better click quality matters more than raw click count.

sections.title: Read annual cost with claim scope
sections.summary: The cheapest product is not automatically the right one.
paragraphs: A low annual burden does not rescue a weak claim set, and a strong listing record does not excuse an unrealistic ownership model. The compare works when both layers remain visible at once.
That is why this page sits alongside, not above, the certification-focused compare surfaces.
bullets: The page exists to hold both truths together.

Public-water households need a compare page that opens after evidence, not before it.
This lane blocks whole-house overreach by keeping the compare set anchored to point-of-use.
whatThisDoesNotTellYou: This page does not replace the utility record.
It does not prove every certified model fits every public-water route.
It does not justify whole-house treatment.

Utility records are the first evidence layer for public-water households.
A narrower compare lane improves both conversion quality and route discipline.

sections.title: Why this compare page exists after the utility read
sections.summary: A public-water household often needs one more step after interpretation: a tighter compare set, not a wider search.
paragraphs: Once the utility route is interpretable, the question usually becomes which certified point-of-use class stays proportionate to the current objective. That is a better question than which product page feels most serious.
This compare page exists to keep that second step narrow. It treats the utility record as the opener and the model compare as the follow-through.

sections.title: What a public-water compare should reward
sections.summary: Claim fit and upkeep quality should decide the shortlist more than prestige or loud marketing.
paragraphs: A stronger public-water compare page should make it easy to eliminate the wrong ownership burden early. Some households need simpler direct-connect or under-sink carbon options. Others will tolerate RO ownership. The route should make that distinction visible.
That means certification, annual cost, and installation class stay on the same screen as the click path.
bullets: The right model is often the narrowest defendable fit.

sections.title: Why this lane helps search and trust
sections.summary: Search users often want a filter answer when they really need a post-record compare lane.
paragraphs: That mismatch is what weak sites exploit. This page turns the same query into a calmer decision step by assuming the utility record already happened and by refusing to widen the scope beyond point-of-use.
It is commercial, but it stays bounded by the same evidence posture as the rest of the engine.
bullets: It answers a buying query without skipping interpretation.
It improves merchant click quality by keeping the route explicit.

sections.summary: A private-well owner should not need to re-decide whether testing mattered before comparing the shortlist.

sections.title: What private-well owners need from the shortlist
sections.summary: Owner-managed routes reward simplicity and upkeep honesty more than badge theater.

That is why annualized cost, replacement cadence, and installation tolerance sit beside the claim support.
bullets: A point-of-use lane should stay narrower than a whole-home pitch.

sections.title: How this compare page protects the route

This is the exact kind of compare page that supports trust instead of spending it.
bullets: Whole-house still requires a separate objective.

comparePages.title: NSF 53 vs 58 claim examples matter more than the badge shorthand
comparePages.lede: This compare page uses real listing examples to show why standard shorthand is only useful when tied to the exact model and claim record.
comparePages.nextActionTitle: Read the exact model and claim before trusting the standard shorthand
comparePages.nextActionSummary: A useful certification compare shows actual products and the claim context they carry. It does not turn 53 or 58 into a stand-alone verdict.
comparisonAxes: How shorthand can mislead when detached from the listing
whyThis: Users often search the badge shorthand before they understand the model-level record.
A compare page with real product examples is more defensible than generic standard lore.
The engine needs a direct answer to the NSF 53 versus 58 query cluster.
whatThisDoesNotTellYou: This page does not claim every 53 product equals every 58 product.
It does not say a 58 badge alone proves a household fit.
It does not collapse EPA guidance into a single shorthand rule.
evidenceHighlights: EPA filter guidance warns against assuming certification automatically means current EPA benchmark compliance.
Listing directories and exact performance records are the right layer for this comparison.
Comparing claim examples is stronger than repeating badge shorthand.

sections.title: Badge shorthand is where users start not where the engine should stop
sections.summary: NSF 53 and 58 are useful because they help users frame the category, but the shorthand becomes misleading when it replaces exact model review.
paragraphs: The compare page fixes that by attaching the shorthand to actual products and claim records. That way the user sees what kind of system carries the standard, what the ownership model looks like, and why the badge alone is not the whole story.
This is also the safest commercial posture for a YMYL-adjacent product question because it keeps the claim record visible.
bullets: Badge shorthand is a starting point.
Model-level claim review is the real work.
A compare page can answer the query without oversimplifying it.

sections.title: 53 and 58 usually map to different product expectations
sections.summary: In practice the user often wants to know what system class usually sits behind the shorthand.
paragraphs: A 53-driven lane often surfaces carbon or other non-RO systems that stay simpler in ownership. A 58-driven lane often surfaces RO systems that ask more of the household. That does not make one badge better. It makes them different commercial paths.
Showing that difference with real examples is more helpful than a bare standards explainer.
bullets: The right badge still depends on the route and the exact model.

sections.title: Why this compare page matters for conversion quality
sections.summary: Users searching certification shorthand are often close to a click, but still need a grounded explanation.
paragraphs: If the page resolves the shorthand question with real product examples and a clear explanation of why the badge is not enough by itself, the user gets both trust and a cleaner route into the product lane.
That is the kind of money page the project should want: explicit, defensible, and tied to actual listing evidence.
bullets: Move the user into the right product lane, not just any click.
